init.title: Luxtronik 2.0 — Settings
init.description: Choose what you want to configure. Smart Energy features (Solar Boost, Night Pause) and Bath Boost are optional — skip them if you don't need them.

data.section: What do you want to configure?

connection.title: Connection Settings
connection.description: Configure how the integration connects to your Luxtronik 2.0 controller.

data_description.host: Local IP address of the Luxtronik 2.0 controller (e.g. 192.168.1.100)
data_description.poll_interval: How often to read data from the heat pump (10–300 seconds, default: 30)

solar_boost.title: Solar Boost
solar_boost.description:
**What it does:** When your solar system feeds more than a configurable amount of power into the grid, the hot water setpoint is automatically raised to store the surplus energy in your buffer tank. Once the feed-in drops, the setpoint returns to normal after a minimum runtime.

**Designed for:** Heat pumps with multi-layer buffer tanks and domestic hot water (DHW) heat exchangers. The idea is to use free solar energy to heat your hot water storage instead of exporting it to the grid.

**How to use:** Enter the entity ID of your grid power sensor. Convention: positive = consumption, negative = feed-in. Leave the sensor field empty to disable this feature entirely.

data_description.grid_sensor: Entity ID of your grid power sensor, e.g. sensor.grid_total. Convention: positive = consumption (buying from grid), negative = feed-in (selling to grid). Leave empty to disable Solar Boost.
data_description.solar_threshold: Solar Boost activates when grid feed-in exceeds this value. Example: 1500 means the heat pump only starts heating when you export more than 1500 W.
data_description.solar_normal_temp: The normal hot water setpoint used when Solar Boost is not active. This is the temperature your hot water tank is normally heated to.
data_description.solar_boost_temp: The raised hot water setpoint during Solar Boost. This should not exceed your controller's maximum allowed hot water temperature.
data_description.solar_min_runtime: Once Solar Boost activates, it stays active for at least this many minutes — even if clouds reduce feed-in temporarily. This prevents the heat pump from cycling on/off rapidly.

night_pause.title: Night Heating Pause
night_pause.description:
**What it does:** Automatically turns off floor heating during the night and restores it in the morning.

**Why this matters:** In systems with a multi-layer buffer tank and DHW heat exchanger, the floor heating circuit can cool down the buffer tank overnight. This means there may not be enough hot water in the morning. By pausing the floor heating at night, the buffer tank stays warm for your morning shower.

**How it works:** During the configured time window, the heating mode is set to 'Off'. Outside the window, it is restored to 'Automatic'.

data_description.night_pause_start: Time when floor heating is turned off. Example: 18:00 means heating stops at 6 PM.
data_description.night_pause_end: Time when floor heating is restored to automatic mode. Example: 09:00 means heating resumes at 9 AM.

bath_boost.title: Bath Boost
bath_boost.description:
**What it does:** Press the Bath Boost button to heat your hot water tank to a higher target temperature for a hot bath. Once the target is reached, the setpoint automatically returns to normal.

**How it works:** Activates Party mode on the hot water circuit (forces immediate heating) and raises the setpoint. A status sensor shows progress and estimated remaining time.

**Tip:** The default target (65.0 °C) works well for most buffer tanks. Lower it if your controller's maximum is below 65 °C.

data_description.bath_boost_target_temp: The temperature to heat the hot water tank to when Bath Boost is activated. Default: 65.0 °C.
data_description.bath_boost_normal_temp: The temperature to restore after Bath Boost completes. This is your normal hot water setpoint. Default: 55.5 °C.

dashboard_info.title: Dashboard Setup
dashboard_info.description:
Ready-to-use dashboards are included in two languages. Pick the one matching your HA language.

**How to set it up:**
1. Go to **Settings → Dashboards → Add Dashboard**
2. Choose a name (e.g. 'Heat Pump') and icon (mdi:heat-pump)
3. Open the new dashboard → **⋮** → **Edit Dashboard** → **⋮** → **Raw configuration editor**
4. Copy the YAML from one of the links below and paste it
5. Replace `your-heatpump-ip` with your heat pump's IP address
6. Click **Save**, then **Done**

**English Dashboard:** {dashboard_en_url}

**German Dashboard (Deutsch):** {dashboard_de_url}

error.cannot_connect: Cannot connect to the heat pump. Check the IP address and make sure the controller is reachable on port 8889.
